text: Hello everyone and welcome to the final part of the laparoscopic mesometrial resection which is a therapeutic common iliac lymphadenectomy. We have described all the other parts and in this part we are going to go into great detailing on the right side and some detailing on the left side for the common iliac lymphadenectomy.

presacral basin accounts for approximately ten percent of the lymphatic drainage occurring in the pelvis and it definitely needs to be removed as far as the therapeutic lymphadenectomy is concerned initially part of the video we are going to be demonstrating the therapeutic lymphadenectomy of the presacral basin the presacral basin is the area between the two common iliac arteries sacral promontory and the hypogastric nerves.
The surgeon carefully lifts the inferior hypogastric nerves to liberate the underlying fibrofatty tissue, facilitating complete clearance of the presacral basin.
The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory below, with visible periosteum, and begins liberating the sticky fibrofatty tissue from the surrounding structures.

I would like to specify the significance and importance of using basic instruments like the laparoscopic shearer and the Bissinger bipolar as you can notice. These two instruments are more than enough for performing the entire surgical proceedings.
If anyone has followed the previous four parts of the video, you will try and realize that the entire surgical proceedings are happening on absolutely simple instruments.
The surgeon uses the Bissinger bipolar device to grasp and coagulate the well-dissected tissue, while the laparoscopic shearer is employed for cutting using bipolar current.
The surgeon carefully uproots and dissects the fibrofatty tissue from the underlying left common iliac vein, taking care to avoid damaging the vein during the procedure.
The surgeon locates the sacral promontory beneath the fibrofatty tissue, using tactile sensation to lift and liberate the fibrofatty tissue from the underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies and coagulates feeder vessels within the surgical plane to maintain a bloodless field during the hysterectomy procedure.

There can be a lot of lymphatic leakage but if you see this entire thing is being presented unedited so you will realize how less do we have to use the suction in order to keep the field dry and clean. Going slow and going gentle is the fundamental principle of achieving therapeutic complication of a surgery in a rapid manner.
The dissection has to be precise, well thought of and executed with perfection with a thorough knowledge of anatomy. the assistant is going to be doing the key steps of providing traction.

Sometimes in obese patient one can cause a permanent traction on the sigmoid with the help of a T-lift device if one wants to use that. However, in this patient we have not used the T-lift device. If somebody wants to wish to use the T-lift device in order to achieve the proceedings with limited traction that is absolutely possible, convenient and good to use. However, we have not done that in this case.
The surgeon liberates a large bulk of the presacral basin, revealing bare surrounding structures, highlighting the importance of thorough dissection in this area.

The pathologist is going to really appreciate when a large chunk of tissue is provided for histopathological examination. It gives them tremendous accuracy in detecting metastasis as well as in keeping a lymph node count which is very important when one is performing a therapeutic common iliac or a therapeutic pelvic lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control minor blood seepage and lymphatic leakage. A large chunk of tissue is liberated from the presacral basin, marking the initial step of a therapeutic common iliac lymphadenectomy for histopathological examination.
The surgeon focuses on performing a common iliac lymphadenectomy on the right side, clearing the lymphatic basin located beneath the venous channels.
The surgeon performs dissection using a laparoscopic shearer through the middle port, with visible precision and control in the unedited segment of the video.
This is the surgical technique which I have developed by using the middle port. And I think it is an extremely convenient and easy to replicate technique.

The surgeon focuses on clearing the upper basin, with the J incision already made to retract the iliac and jejunal plexus. The assistant uses non-graspable firm traction with a needle holder to maneuver the ureter away from the mesourator.

The surgeon uses traction to expose the common iliac basin, with the ureter serving as the lateral border of the dissection. The tract is being cleared to achieve therapeutic clearance of the common iliac basin.

The surgeon carefully delineates the surgical plane on the right side, exposing the underlying inferior vena cava and its perforator vessels, ensuring these structures are visible and protected during the dissection.

The surgeon releases fibrofatty tissue and the mesourator, exposing a large chunk of tissue for liberation. The inferior vena cava is visible with two or three perforator vessels, requiring careful dissection to prevent hemorrhage.

The surgeon begins the procedure by exposing the psoas muscle, revealing the fibro-fatty tissue beneath it. This tissue extends underneath the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the right common iliac vein, highlighting the presence of one to two perforating vessels in the area.
The surgeon liberates the tissue completely for therapeutic lymph node clearance, ensuring no tissue is left behind. The excised tissue is prepared for histopathological examination.
The surgeon carefully spares the nerves while dissecting the fibro fatty tissue, staying flush to the vein to facilitate gentle and effective mobilization of the vein.
The surgeon identifies and safely coagulates small perforating vessels while dissecting lymph nodes located beneath the common iliac artery and at the beginning of the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon proceeds from lateral to medial, transitioning from dissecting venous channels to arterial channels, and then to the presacral basin, achieving a thorough clearance of the surgical area.

A good head low is important. We prefer to use the three D laparoscopy in order to perform these dissections. One may do these dissections equally brilliantly even on the standard HD camera equipment which is available as the depth is not very important because the surface is very close to the camera. It is not in the depth of the pelvis.
The surgeon carefully plucks apart the inter arteriovenous lymphatic channels, ensuring these channels are bare to liberate the lymphatic tissue completely.
The surgeon completes the dissection and removes the entire package of lymph nodes, preparing it for histopathological examination.

The surgeon continues the dissection until reaching the left common iliac vein, using the Bisinger and sharer instruments to de-roof fibro-fatty tissue overlying the vein. The technique allows for simultaneous tissue separation and coagulation, revealing the left common iliac vein beneath the right common iliac artery.

The surgeon de-roofs the area around the left common iliac vein, revealing the vein completely. The lymphatic tissue is removed as a single package in an en bloc dissection, ensuring the entire chunk is taken out intact. The dissection continues into the presacral basin.

The surgeon completes the dissection within five minutes, maintaining a bloodless surgical field. Minimal bleeding is managed with suction, revealing clear tissue planes. Fluid accumulation is visible in free spaces, addressed with suction irrigation.

The surgeon prepares the left common iliac basin for lymphadenectomy, exposing the psoas muscle on the left side. The ureter is visible below the muscle, and the left common iliac artery is identified alongside the hypogastric plexus and the right common iliac artery. The surgical field is clear, allowing for a tension-free dissection.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery forming the roof of the dissection area. The psoas muscle and ureter are clearly visible. A small chunk of fibro fatty tissue is prepared for removal as part of the left common iliac lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic needle holder to lift the ureter, exposing the psoas muscle beneath. Suctioning is performed to maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon prepares the lymphatic tissue at the level of the left common iliac artery, carefully liberating the entire package while avoiding perforator vessels. The fibro fatty tissue appears slippery, requiring careful handling. The bifurcation of the aorta is clearly visible, and the package surrounding the left common iliac artery is gently liberated.

The surgeon applies a small suction device to clear accumulated blood from the surgical field, revealing the exposed psoas muscle and the lymphatic package that needs to be removed. The basin appears bloodless, with no active bleeding observed.
The surgeon carefully dissects and uplifts the lymphatic package near the left common iliac artery, ensuring gentle traction to avoid damaging the numerous perforator vessels in the area.
The surgeon navigates around perforator vessels to prevent inadvertent bleeding while dissecting sticky lymphatic tissue, which is tougher than typical pelvic tissue.

The surgeon liberates tissue and coagulates a small damaged perforator vessel, which caused a minor spurt of blood. A moist gauze piece is used to maintain a clear surgical plane, aiding in the identification of perforator vessels. The spurt results in a minor hemorrhage of approximately two to three milliliters of blood.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the lymphatic tissue plane near the left common iliac artery using bipolar forceps for coagulation. The tissue package is carefully liberated without stretching or pulling, and is prepared for histopathological examination, marking the completion of the therapeutic common iliac lymphadenectomy on this side.

The surgeon performs mesometrial dissection with clear visibility, systematically removing fibrofatty tissue from the surgical field, leaving the basins free of such tissue, completing the therapeutic dissection on the left side.
